coredata.dc:description: Herein, the multifunctional carbon nanodots (CDs)-decorated plasmonic Au@Ag nanoenzymes coupled with Ti2C/Ag2S composites were proposed for photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensing of microRNA. Two-dimensional (2D) Ti2C MXenes-sensitized Ag2S quantum dots (QDs) (Ti2C/Ag2S composites) acquired an initial photocurrent. After immobilization of a hairpin subsidiary probe (S1) onto the Ti2C/Ag2S composites photoelectrode surface, the target microRNA-associated bipedal DNA walker performed the opening of S1 for forming triple helix molecules. Then the CDs-decorated Au@Ag core–shell (Au@Ag@CDs) nanocuboids were introduced into the PEC biosensing platform through target-induced triple helix molecules. The Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids as a multifunctional signal amplifier not only competitively captured the irradiating light and electron donors to weaken the PEC signal, but also quenched the photocurrent response resulting from energy transfer between plasmonic bimetallic Au@Ag nanocuboids and Ag2S QDs. Additionally, the Au@Ag and CDs in Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids displayed a synergistic catalytic effect, and could serve as peroxidase nanoenzymes to catalyze the production of precipitates onto the biosensor surface, resulting in a significantly decreased photocurrent. Based on the plasmonic Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes coupled with Ti2C/Ag2S composites, microRNA-155 was ultrasensitively detected with a wide linear range (1–10,000 fM) and a low limit of detection (0.83 fM). Moreover, the engineered plasmonic Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes offer an environmentally friendly and multifunctional signal amplifier for fabricating PEC biosensor and have potential applications in biomarkers detection and disease diagnostics.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) Au nanorods, (B) Au@Ag nanocuboids, (C) CDs and (D) Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids. (E) HRTEM image of Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids. (F) UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra of (a) Au nanorods, (b) Au@Ag nanocuboids, (c) CDs and (d) Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids.

(A) TEM image, (B) XRD spectrum and (C) Ti 2p and C 1s XPS spectra of Ti2C MXenes. (D) TEM image and (E) XPS spectra of Ag 3d and S 2p of Ag2S QDs. (F) UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra of (a) Ag2S QDs, (b) Ti2C MXenes and (c) Ti2C/Ag2S composites.

(A) Photocurrents of (a) ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S electrode, (b) ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S/S1/BSA/F1 + F2 + microRNA+S2 electrode after incubation with Au@Ag@CDs, (c) ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S/S1/BSA/F1 + F2 + microRNA+S2/Au@Ag@CDs electrode after reaction with the mixture of 10 mM 4-CN and 1 mM H2O2. (B) Photocurrents of (a) ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S/S1/BSA/F1 + F2 + microRNA+S2 electrode, (b) ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S/S1/BSA/F1 + F2 + microRNA+S2 electrode after incubation with Au@Ag.

(A) EIS (performed in 5 mM (1:1) [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution containing 0.1 M KCl at the frequency ranging from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz with 5 mV as the amplitude) and (B) PEC investigation (performed in 0.1 M Tris–HCl (pH 7.4) containing 0.1 M AA at 0 V bias): (a) ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S, (b) ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S/S1, (c) ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S/S1/BSA, (d) ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S/S1/BSA/F1 + F2 + microRNA+S2, (e) ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S/S1/F1 + F2 + microRNA+S2 after reaction with Au@Ag@CDs, (f) ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S/S1/F1 + F2 + microRNA+S2/Au@Ag@CDs after reaction with the mixture of 10 mM 4-CN and 1 mM H2O2.

The effects of (A) mass ratio of Ti2C MXenes/Ag2S QDs, (B) bipedal DNA walker reaction time (1:1 mass ratio of Ti2C MXenes/Ag2S QDs, 1 μM S1 concentration, 1000 fM microRNA-155 concentration, 90 min Au@Ag@CDs incubation time), (C) Au@Ag@CDs incubation time (90 min bipedal DNA walker reaction time), (D) deposition time (90 min Au@Ag@CDs incubation time) on photocurrents of the PEC biosensor. Error bars stand for the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

(A) The photocurrent responses of different-concentration microRNA-155 (from a to i) 1, 2, 10, 20, 100, 200, 1000, 2000, and 10,000 fM, and (B) the corresponding calibration curve. (C) Selectivity of the PEC biosensor. The interfering microRNAs concentrations were 100,000 fM and target microRNA-155 was 2000 fM, and the mixture was 100,000 fM interferences and 2000 fM microRNA-155. Error bars stand for the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

(A) Schematic for the preparation of Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids. (B) Plasmonic Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes-driven multifunctional signal amplification combined with Ti2C/Ag2S composites for PEC biosensing. (C) The possible photocurrent signal amplification mechanism of the PEC biosensor.
Recovery tests of microRNA-155 (n = 3) in the human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Engineering of multifunctional carbon nanodots-decorated plasmonic Au@Ag nanoenzymes for photoelectrochemical biosensing of microRNA-155

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the multifunctional carbon nanodots (CDs)-decorated plasmonic Au@Ag nanoenzymes coupled with Ti2C/Ag2S composites were proposed for photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensing of microRNA. Two-dimensional (2D) Ti2C MXenes-sensitized Ag2S quantum dots (QDs) (Ti2C/Ag2S composites) acquired an initial photocurrent. After immobilization of a hairpin subsidiary probe (S1) onto the Ti2C/Ag2S composites photoelectrode surface, the target microRNA-associated bipedal DNA walker performed the opening of S1 for forming triple helix molecules. Then the CDs-decorated Au@Ag core–shell (Au@Ag@CDs) nanocuboids were introduced into the PEC biosensing platform through target-induced triple helix molecules. The Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids as a multifunctional signal amplifier not only competitively captured the irradiating light and electron donors to weaken the PEC signal, but also quenched the photocurrent response resulting from energy transfer between plasmonic bimetallic Au@Ag nanocuboids and Ag2S QDs. Additionally, the Au@Ag and CDs in Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids displayed a synergistic catalytic effect, and could serve as peroxidase nanoenzymes to catalyze the production of precipitates onto the biosensor surface, resulting in a significantly decreased photocurrent. Based on the plasmonic Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes coupled with Ti2C/Ag2S composites, microRNA-155 was ultrasensitively detected with a wide linear range (1–10,000 fM) and a low limit of detection (0.83 fM). Moreover, the engineered plasmonic Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes offer an environmentally friendly and multifunctional signal amplifier for fabricating PEC biosensor and have potential applications in biomarkers detection and disease diagnostics.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensor, as an emerging and developing analytical technology in biomarkers detection, has received significant attention owing to its high sensitivity, operational simplicity, and easy miniaturization [1–8]. Various signal amplification mechanisms have been utilized for the construction of high-performance PEC biosensors, such as enzymatic reactions, energy transfer, and steric hindrance effects [9–11]. In particular, the enzymatic reactions-based biocatalytic precipitation strategy has been widely implemented for attenuating the PEC response due to its high specificity and catalytic activity [12–14]. However, natural enzymes generally suffer from several disadvantages, such as poor stability, high cost, and low catalytic activity easily caused by external conditions [15,16]. Recently, nanoenzymes, as a generation of artificial enzymes with highly effective enzyme-like properties, which possess the advantages of convenience in preparation and extraordinary stability in harsh environments, have been exploited for designing a satisfactory PEC biosensor [17,18]. Carbon nanodots (CDs), an example from the burgeoning field of environmentally friendly carbon-based nanomaterials, exhibit intrinsic peroxidase-like activity, which can catalyze the oxidation of 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine in the presence of H2O2 [19]. Additionally, as a metal-free photoactive material, CDs display unique electron transfer properties, photostability and facile functionalization, thereby enabling their wide application in biosensing [20,21]. Hence, development of powerful CDs-based nanoenzymes is being actively pursued.

Energy transfer (ET) between noble Au or Ag nanomaterials and quantum dots (QDs) has also been employed as a unique phenomenon to quench the PEC photocurrent [22,23]. Moreover, Au or Ag nanomaterials exhibit distinctive characteristics, such as unique local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR), environmental safety, excellent peroxidase-like activity towards H2O2 reduction and versatile surface functionalities [24–27]. Nevertheless, compared with the single Au or Ag NP-driven LSPR effect, Au–plasmonic bimetallic nanostructures, such as Au@Ag core–are capable of exhibiting broader resonance range [28]. Considering that doping with noble metallic nanomaterials can enhance the catalytic activity of a single nanoenzyme, engineering a plasmonic nanoenzyme combining Au@Ag core–shell and CDs as a potential PEC biosensing amplifier is highly desirable.

Also, Ti2C MXene as a family of two-dimensional (2D) transition-metal material, has become a promising light-absorbing material for use PEC biosensors because it can be combined with semiconductors to enhance the electrons-holes separation efficiency [29–31]. Ag2S QDs are good candidates for constructing PEC biosensing platforms, due to their suitable NIR emissions, water solubility, and low toxicity [32]. In fact, Ag2S QDs with a narrow band gap can withstand a rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons-holes [33]. Considering these characteristics of Ti2C MXenes and Ag2S QDs, Ti2C/Ag2S composites are expected to display high photoelectric conversion efficiencies.

MicroRNA-155 is closely related to the occurrence and development of cancer, which as an attractive biomarker plays an important role in the early diagnosis and prognostic treatment of cancer [34–36]. Herein, based on plasmonic Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes-driven multifunctional signal amplification coupled with Ti2C/Ag2S composites, an environmentally friendly PEC biosensor was proposed for microRNA-155 determination ( Scheme 1). As shown in Scheme 1, Ti2C/Ag2S composites displayed excellent photocurrent signals due to their high photon-electron conversion efficiency. Plasmonic Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes as a multifunctional signal amplifier were designed by loading CDs onto the surface of Au@Ag nanocuboids and introduced into the biosensing platform through target microRNA-induced bipedal DNA walker amplification strategy. First, the plasmonic Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids quenched the photocurrent signal because of the energy transfer between bimetallic Au@Ag nanocuboids and Ag2S QDs. Secondly, Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids competitively captured the irradiating light and electron donors to weaken the PEC signal. Additionally, due to the synergistic catalytic effect of Au@Ag and CDs, the Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes efficiently catalyzed 4-chloro-1-naphthol (4-CN) oxidation by H2O2 to generate 4-chloro-hexadienone (4-CD) on the biosensor, leading to a significantly decreased photocurrent. Thus, the combination of plasmonic Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes and Ti2C/Ag2S composites, the microRNA-155 was detected with a wide linear response (1–10,000 fM) and a low limit of detection (0.83 fM). Furthermore, the designed plasmonic Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes acted as a novel signal amplifier, and would be beneficial for fabricating PEC biosensors for biomarker detection.
Materials and apparatus used in this study are detailed in the Supporting Information.
The detail of Ti2C/Ag2S composites preparation is included in the Supporting Information.

Au nanorods and Au@Ag core–shell nanocuboids were synthesized according to the previously reported paper [37,38]. Specially, an ice-cold sodium borohydride (NaBH4) solution (0.01 M, 0.6 mL) was poured quickly into the mixture of chloroauric acid (HAuCl4, 0.01 M, 0.25 mL) and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, 0.1 M, 10 mL) to prepare Au seeds. Then, HAuCl4 (0.01 M, 5 mL) and silver nitrate (AgNO3, 0.01 M, 1 mL) were mixed with CTAB (0.1 M, 95 mL), followed by the addition of ascorbic acid (AA, 0.1 M, 0.55 mL). Subsequently, the Au seeds (0.12 mL) were added into the above mixture to synthesize Au nanorods. And the solution was gently mixed for 20 s and allowed to stand for at least 12 h before use. Then, the Au nanorods were centrifuged and re-dispersed in water. The above Au nanorods (50 mL) were dispersed in cetyltrimethylammonium chloride (CTAC) aqueous solution (0.08 M, 50 mL) and kept for at least 12 h to ensure that CTAB was completely replaced by CTAC. Next, AgNO3 (0.01 M, 5 mL) was injected into the above solution under agitation, followed by AA addition (0.1 M, 2.5 mL). The obtained solution was kept in an isothermal oven at 65 °C for 3 h to synthesize the Au@Ag core–shell nanocuboids. Finally, the Au@Ag nanocuboids were centrifuged twice and re-dispersed in water.

CDs were synthesized according to the previously reported procedure [39]. Briefly, citric acid (0.42 g) and ethylenediamine (0.536 mL) were dissolved in water (10 mL). The mixture was placed into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (30 mL) and heated at 200 ºC for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the CD solution was obtained following membrane filtration (0.22 µm). To prepare Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids, the above CDs (2 mL) were added into as-synthesized Au@Ag nanocuboids suspension (1 mL), and the mixture was shaken gently for 16 h to allow the formation of AgN bonds between the Au@Ag nanocuboids and the CDs NH2 groups [40,41]. The composite nanomaterials were then centrifuged and washed. Finally, the obtained Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids were re-dispersed in 1 mL water and stored at 4 °C till further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the PEC biosensor and PEC assay
ce:section.ce:para: First, an indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode was cleaned by ultrasonic treatment with 1 M NaOH, 1 M NaOH in ethanol/water (1:1, v/v) and deionized water successively for 20 min [42]. Then, 25 μL of Ti2C/Ag2S composites were dropped onto the surface of ITO electrode and dried naturally to obtain the ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S electrode. Next, 20 μL of EDC/NHS was dropped onto the electrode for 15 min to activate the -COOH groups of the Ti2C/Ag2S composites [43]. A hairpin subsidiary probe (S1, 1 μM, 20 μL) was heated to 95 °C for 5 min and cooled naturally to room temperature, then assembled onto the modified electrode surface for 2 h. Subsequently, the obtained ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S/S1 electrode was immersed in bovine serum albumin (BSA) solution (1 wt%) for 30 min to block the non-specific binding sites [44,45]. For electrode modification, 20 μL of mixture containing subsidiary probe (S2, 1.5 μM), bipedal DNA foot 1 (F1, 1 μM), bipedal DNA foot 2 (F2, 1 μM) and different-concentration microRNA-155 were incubated for 60 min, and then transferred to the electrode surface for 90 min at 37 °C to form triple helix molecules between S1 and S2. Next, the biosensing platform was placed in the Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboid solution (20 μL) for 90 min at 37 °C via the covalent interactions between the NH2 groups of S2 probes and the -COOH groups on the CDs [45,46]. The obtained electrode was then reacted with a mixture of 10 mM 4-CN and 1 mM H2O2 for 20 min. Finally, a PEC assay was performed in Tris–HCl (pH 7.4, 0.1 M) containing AA (0.1 M) at 0 bias under λ > 420 nm illumination. AA as an electron donor could effectively scavenge the photogenerated holes and facilitate the transfer of photogenerated electrons, resulting in an enhanced photocurrent response [47,48]. Also, an applied potential of 0 V was used to facilitate the injection of photogenerated electrons of the PEC system into the ITO electrode [49,50]. Meanwhile, a UV cut-off filter was used to provide visible light of λ > 420 nm because the UV light may inevitably cause the damage of biomolecules [51,52].

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the Au nanorods, Au@Ag nanocuboids and CDs were displayed in Fig. 1A–C. Notably, the Au nanorods possessed uniform size distributions (Fig. 1A). After the Au nanorods cores were fully enwrapped by uniform Ag shells, the obtained Au@Ag products showed the core–shell nanocuboids with an average length and diameter of 75 nm and 55 nm, respectively (Fig. 1B). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. S1) showed that the Au@Ag nanocuboids were well crystalized in a lattice with a spacing of 0.209 nm, which ascribed to the (200) lattice planes of Ag [53]. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis results further confirmed the successful preparation of Au@Ag nanocuboids (Fig. S2). The Au@Ag nanocuboids with four diffraction peaks were located at ~38.4, 44.5, 64.0 and 77.6°, corresponding to Ag or Au(111), (200), (220) and (331) facets, respectively [38]. As shown in Fig. 1C, the CDs were mono-dispersed with a typical particle size of 4.5 nm, and exhibited a lattice spacing of 0.232 nm [54]. To further study the structural characteristics of the CDs, they were analyzed by XRD (Fig. S3). The XRD pattern contained a broad diffraction peak at 23.8°, indicating that the CDs were amorphous due to disordered carbon atoms [55]. Moreover, the FT-IR spectrum of the CDs was obtained (Fig. S4). As shown in Fig. S4, the two strong peaks at 3268 and 1638 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of the -OH/-NH group and the CO bond, respectively, indicating that CDs contained abundant -NH2 and -COOH groups [56], which promoted binding with the Au@Ag nanocuboids and S2 probes.

After decoration with CDs, although the as-formed Au@Ag@CDs nanohybrids retained a nanocuboid structure, many nanoparticles appeared on the surface of Au@Ag nanocuboids (Fig. 1D). The HRTEM image (Fig. 1E) confirmed that the nanoparticle lattice fringes were 0.232 nm long corresponding to CDs, and the interplanar spacing of 0.209 nm was assigned to Ag (200). Furthermore, the UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra of these samples were shown in Fig. 1F. Notably, the Au nanorods (curve a) displayed two characteristic absorption peaks at 511 and 780 nm, ascribed to the transverse and longitudinal plasmon resonance wavelengths, respectively [57]. After the Ag shell was formed, the characteristic absorption peaks of the Au nanorods disappeared, and four characteristic absorption peaks of Au@Ag nanocuboids (curve b) were observed at 350, 390, 449 and 488 nm, with the first two corresponding to the longitudinal and transverse dipolar plasmon modes, and the latter two to the octupolar plasmon mode, respectively [38]. The CDs (curve c) gave rise to a typical absorption peak at 349 nm [39]. Compared with Au@Ag nanocuboids, this typical CD absorption was observed for the Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids (curve d), indicating that the CDs were successfully decorated on the surface of the Au@Ag nanocuboids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Ti2C MXenes and Ag2S QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The microstructure of the as-synthesized Ti2C MXenes was characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2A, Ti2C MXenes exhibited an ultrathin flake-like structure, suggesting the successful exfoliation of single-layered Ti2C MXenes. To confirm the successful preparation of Ti2C MXenes, they were analyzed using XRD and the resulting XRD pattern is shown in Fig. 2B. A distinct characteristic peak was visible at 2θ ≈ 6.8°, which was assigned to (002), indicating the successful synthesis of Ti2C MXenes [58,59]. Furthermore, the Ti2C MXenes were analyzed by XPS to determine the elemental compositions and states. As displayed in Fig. S5, the survey spectrum of Ti2C MXenes confirmed the existence of Ti, C, O and F elements. Moreover, the Ti 2p peaks (Fig. 2C) were located at 454.5 and 462.2 eV corresponding to Ti–C, and the other two peaks (458.2 and 464.5 eV) were ascribed to Ti–O [60,61]. Similarly, the C 1 s peaks (Fig. 2C) could be decomposed into four peaks, confirming the existence of C–Ti (281.0 eV), C–Ti–O (282.1 eV), C–C (284.8 eV), and CO (286.42 eV), respectively [62]. The abundant oxygen-containing functional groups promoted Ti2C MXenes decoration of Ag2S QDs and the dispersion of Ti2C/Ag2S composites in water [43,63].

On the other hand, the TEM image shown in Fig. 2D indicated that the as-prepared Ag2S QDs were monodisperse nanoparticles, approximately 2 nm in size. XRD analysis of Ag2S QDs did not give rise to a distinctive XRD pattern, possibly due to the small size of Ag2S QDs (Fig. S6), which was consistent with previously reported results [64]. From Fig. 2E, the Ag 3d spectrum was deconvoluted into two peaks at 368.0 and 374.0 eV, which were assigned to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 for Ag+ in the sample. Similarly, the S 2p peaks (Fig. 2E) at 161.1 eV (S 2p3/2) and 162.2 eV (S 2p1/2) were ascribed to be S2-, confirming the formation of Ag2S [53]. Additionally, the UV–vis-NIR absorption spectra of Ti2C MXenes, Ag2S QDs and Ti2C/Ag2S composites were shown in Fig. 2F. Notably, the Ag2S QDs gave rise to a prominent peak at 819 nm, suggesting excellent light absorption properties (curve a). Ti2C MXenes showed a wide absorption range (curve b). After the introduction of Ti2C MXenes, the light absorption capacity of the Ti2C/Ag2S composites was superior to that of pure Ag2S QDs (curve c). Moreover, the fluorescence emission spectrum of Ti2C/Ag2S composites was obtained (Fig. S7). The fluorescence emission intensity of Ti2C/Ag2S composites (curve b) at 825 nm was weaker than that of Ag2S QDs (curve a), which indicated that Ti2C MXenes could suppress the charges recombination of Ag2S QDs and the Ti2C/Ag2S composites had efficient charge separation [32,43]. The results suggested that Ti2C/Ag2S composites could be used as an effective photoactive material for PEC biosensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of plasmonic Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes-driven multifunctional signal amplification
ce:section.ce:para: To explore plasmonic Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes-driven multifunctional signal amplification, the energy levels of Ag2S QDs, Ti2C MXenes, and CDs were characterized by Tauc plots [6,43]. As displayed in Fig S8, the band gaps of Ag2S QDs, Ti2C MXenes, and CDs were 1.10 eV, 2.87 and 2.88 eV, respectively. Moreover, Mott-Schottky (M-S) curves were carried out to evaluate the flat-band potentials of the synthesized materials [65]. Fig S9 demonstrated that Ag2S QDs had a negative slope corresponding to p-type semiconductors and Ti2C MXenes and CDs exhibited positive slopes corresponding to n-type semiconductors. The flat-band potentials of Ag2S QDs, Ti2C MXenes and CDs were 1.26, −0.75 and −0.69 V vs saturated calomel electrode (SCE), which corresponded to 1.50, −0.51 and −0.45 V vs normal hydrogen electrode (NHE), respectively. Because the flat-band potential is 0.1 V higher than the conduction band (CB) potential (for n-type semiconductors) or valence band (VB) potential (for p-type semiconductors) [65,66], the CB values of Ti2C MXenes and CDs were −0.61 and −0.55 V vs NHE, and the VB value of Ag2S QDs was 1.40 V vs NHE. According to the formula the band gaps=VB potential-CB potential [67], the CB value of Ag2S QDs corresponded to 0.30 V, and the CB values of Ti2C MXenes and CDs corresponded to 2.26 and 2.33 V vs NHE, respectively.

Based on the above data, the photocurrents of the various modified electrodes and the corresponding possible mechanisms were investigated in Scheme 1C. To acquire a large initial photocurrent, 2D Ti2C MXenes were taken as ideal supporting materials to sensitize Ag2S QDs, as the excellent electronic conductivity and numerous hydrophilic functions of Ti2C MXenes could effectively promote electrons/holes separation and transformation of Ag2S QDs (Scheme 1C, a). Under light irradiation, the photo-generated electrons transferred from the CB (−0.61 V) of Ti2C MXenes to the CB (0.3 V) of Ag2S QDs, and then transferred to the ITO electrode, and AA was used to consume the photo-generated holes to inhibit the recombination of electron/hole pairs, leading to an excellent photocurrent of ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S electrode ( Fig. 3A, curve a). After the introduction of Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids through target microRNA–induced formation of triple helix molecules, the photocurrent decreased (Fig. 3A, curve b), possibly resulting from not only energy transfer between Ag2S QDs and plasmonic bimetallic Au@Ag nanocuboids, but also that Au@Ag@CDs could competitively capture the irradiating light and electron donors of the PEC system based on their matching energy levels (Scheme 1C, b) [68,69]. To verify that the Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids participate in the quenching of photocurrent signals due to the energy transfer effect, the photocurrents of ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S/S1/BSA/F1 +F2 +microRNA+S2 electrode were investigated before and after incubation with Au@Ag nanocuboids. As shown in Fig. 3B, compared with photocurrent of the ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S/S1/BSA/F1 +F2 +microRNA+S2 electrode (Fig. 3B, curve a), photocurrent of the modified electrode after incubation with Au@Ag nanocuboids became lower (Fig. 3B, curve a), which may be attributed to the energy transfer effect between the Au@Ag nanocuboids and Ag2S QDs [22,70]. When the modified electrode was reacted with the mixture of 4-CN and H2O2, the photocurrent further weakened (Fig. 3A, curve c), because Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes with the synergistic catalytic effect of Au@Ag and CDs could efficiently catalyze the oxidation of 4-CN by H2O2 to produce 4-CD precipitates onto the electrode, which blocked electrons transport (Scheme 1C, c) [10,71]. These results indicated that the plasmonic Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes could be used as a satisfactory multiple signal amplifier for PEC biosensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication characterization of the developed PEC biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the feasibility of the developed biosensor, the formation of DNA composite structures was characterized by performing 15% polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE). As displayed in Fig. S10, distinct S1 and S2 bands were observed in lane 1 and lane 2, respectively. After mixing S1 and S2, two separate bands appeared, which individually corresponded to S1 and S2 (lane 3), indicating that S2 could not open hairpin S1 to enable triple helix molecules formation. Single distinct bands were observed in lanes 4 and 5 corresponding to F1 and F2, respectively. When target microRNA-155 was mixed with S1, F1 and F2, new separate bands were observed and the band of F1 or F2 disappeared in lane 7, due to the fact that microRNA-155 firstly paired with F1 +F2 for opening of S1 which further paired with S1 (the top band). The bottom band may correspond to single S1 or microRNA-155. Then S2 was added into the mixture of S1 and F1 +F2 +microRNA-155, leading to the appearance of new bands (lane 6). The top band was similar to those of the S1 +F1 +F2 +microRNA-155 composite structures. Moreover, the new lower band possibly corresponded to the formation of triple helix molecules between S2 and S1 facilitated by the S1 +F1 +F2 +microRNA-155 composite structures. The PAGE results confirmed that the target microRNA-induced bipedal DNA walker facilitated hairpin S1 opening by S2 to enable the formation of triple helix molecules.

On the other hand, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a useful method for verifying the interface properties of the modified electrode and the corresponding semicircle diameter indicates the interfacial charge-transfer resistance (R
ct ) values. As displayed in Fig. 4A, the ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S electrode gave rise to an obvious R
ct (curve a), due to the efficient blocking effect of semiconductor Ag2S QDs. After the ordinal immobilization of S1 and BSA, due to the poor conductivity of DNA and protein molecules, the R
ct values gradually increased (curve b-c). Furthermore, when the mixed solution of F1, F2, S2 and target microRNA-155 was incubated with the ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S/S1/BSA electrode, the R
ct values increased continuously (curve d), suggesting the steric hindrance effect of the formed triple helix molecules. However, with the introduction of Au@Ag@CDs on the modified electrode surface, the R
ct value decreased (curve e), due to the excellent conductivity of Au@Ag@CDs, which facilitated electron transfer. Nevertheless, the R
ct value increased markedly after reaction with 4-CN and H2O2 mixture (curve f), because Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes could efficiently catalyze 4-CN by H2O2 to generate insoluble 4-CD deposited on the electrode to prevent [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox probe from the electrode. Collectively, these results indicated that the proposed PEC biosensor was successfully fabricated.

The successively modified electrodes were further traced by PEC measurement. Notably, the photocurrents showed sharp peaks with the time during the light-on period due to the consumption of electron donors (AA). Thus, the maximum photocurrent readouts were used throughout. As displayed in Fig. 4B, the ITO/Ti2C/Ag2S electrode exhibited a large photocurrent signal (curve a), because of the outstanding photoelectric conversion efficiency of Ti2C/Ag2S composites. After successive incubation with S1, BSA and a mixture of F1 +F2 +microRNA+S2, the dramatically decreased photocurrents were achieved (curves b-d), which were consistent with those of EIS investigation (Fig. 4A). Subsequently, Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids were introduced into the biosensing platform through formative triple helix molecules, resulting in a significantly quenched photocurrent (curve e), as plasmonic bimetallic Au@Ag nanocuboids induced energy transfer between the Au@Ag nanocuboids and Ag2S QDs, and Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids competitively captured irradiating light and the electron donors of PEC system. Additionally, when this PEC biosensing platform was reacted with the mixture of 4-CN and H2O2, the PEC signal decreased markedly, confirming that the Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes displayed peroxidase activity for promoting 4-CN oxidation into insoluble 4-CD precipitates, which obstructed AA from reaching the electrode surface. These results suggested that plasmonic Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes-based PEC biosensing was successfully performed according to Scheme 1.

To enhance the efficiency of the proposed PEC biosensing system, several experiment parameters were optimized, including the mass ratio of Ti2C MXenes/Ag2S QDs, bipedal DNA walker reaction time, Au@Ag@CDs incubation time and deposition time.

The mass ratios of Ti2C MXenes/Ag2S QDs were optimized to achieve a sufficiently high initial photocurrent ( Fig. 5A). The initial photocurrents increased with increasing Ti2C MXene concentration. As the mass ratio of Ti2C MXenes/Ag2S QDs was 1:1, a relatively large photocurrent was observed. However, at mass ratios higher than 1:1, the photocurrents decreased, due to the fact that a high concentration of Ti2C MXenes was reunited together and increased diffusion resistance of electron motion in this composite. So, 1:1 was chosen as the optimal mass ratio of Ti2C MXenes/Ag2S QDs.

Generally, bipedal DNA walker takes a certain time to open hairpin S1 for forming triple helix molecules with S2. So, the reaction time of bipedal DNA walker was optimized. As shown in Fig. 5B, the photocurrent response promoted with the expansion of reaction time and then reached a plateau at 90 min, therefore, 90 min was considered the optimum reaction time of bipedal DNA walker.

The incubation time of Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids as a key factor was also optimized. From Fig. 5C, much more Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids were introduced on the electrode surface with the prolongation of incubation time, resulting in a decreased photocurrent, probably because Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids could induce the energy transfer and competitively capture the irradiating light and electron donors. A plateau was achieved after 90 min. Therefore, 90 min was taken as the Au@Ag@CDs incubation time.

The 4-CN deposition time was optimized to maximize insoluble 4-CD formation and PEC signal reduction. As displayed in Fig. 5D, with the increased deposition time, the photocurrent decreased gradually, and a plateau was arrived at 20 min. Therefore, 20 min was selected as the 4-CN deposition time.

The high-performance PEC biosensor based on plasmonic Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes coupled with Ti2C/Ag2S composites was developed for the detection of microRNA-155 under optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 6A, the photocurrents decreased with the increase of microRNA-155 concentration (1–10,000 fM). The photocurrent responses were linear with the logarithm of microRNA-155 concentrations in the range from 1 to 10,000 fM (Fig. 6B). The corresponding linear equation was photocurrent (μA)= 10.05–2.36 lgC microRNA (fM) (R
2 = 0.9992), and the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated as 0.83 fM (3σ), which was lower than those of some reported PEC microRNAs detection systems (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the PEC biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the designed PEC biosensing selectivity for microRNA-155 detection, the photocurrent responses towards several possible interfering microRNAs, such as microRNA-141, microRNA-21, microRNA-375 and microRNA-224 were tested, respectively. As displayed in Fig. 6C, the photocurrent responses for interfering microRNAs had no obvious change compared with the blank. Nevertheless, a significantly reduced photocurrent towards microRNA-155 (1000 fM) was achieved, and the photocurrent response towards microRNA-155 was almost the same as the mixture (100,000 fM interferences and 2000 fM microRNA-155), because target microRNA-155 enabled the running of bipedal DNA walker to induce multifunctional signal amplification. This indicated that the biosensor exhibited satisfying selectivity for microRNA-155. The reproducibility of the proposed PEC biosensor was investigated with five independent electrodes. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the photocurrent towards 2000 fM microRNA-155 was 5.4%, revealing excellent reproducibility.

The stability of the reported biosensor was further evaluated. As displayed in Fig. S11, the photocurrent responses of the biosensor towards microRNA-155 (10,000 fM) after consecutive 8 cycles with “off-on-off” light illumination did not change significantly. Additionally, the stability of the biosensor was investigated by storing it at 4 °C in refrigerator for 2 weeks, and the photocurrent could maintain about 93.3% of its initial photocurrent towards microRNA-155 (200 fM). These results showed that the established biosensor had satisfactory stability.

To further evaluate the applicability of the developed PEC biosensor in complex biological samples, the healthy human serum samples from the First Affiliated Hospital of Zhengzhou University (Zhengzhou, China) were selected for analysing microRNA-155. As displayed in Table 1, 2, 10, 100, 1000 and 2000 fM of standard microRNA-155 were separately joined into the 10-fold diluted serum sample (10 mM PBS, pH 7.4), and the tested average and standard deviation of found photocurrents or concentrations were achieved. The obtained average recoveries and RSDs separately were 96.0–106.5%, 2.3–7.6%, indicating that the designed PEC biosensor has potential application in microRNA-155 detection in real samples.

In summary, plasmonic Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes-driven multifunctional signal amplification strategy coupling with Ti2C/Ag2S composites was designed to develop ultrasensitive PEC biosensors for microRNA-155 assay. The Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids as a signal amplifier not only competitively captured the irradiating light and electron donors to weaken the PEC signal, but also quenched the photocurrent because of energy transfer between plasmonic Au@Ag nanocuboids and Ag2S QDs. Additionally, the Au@Ag@CDs nanocuboids as peroxidase nanoenzymes could efficiently produce catalytic precipitates onto the electrode surface to block the PEC process. Benefitting from plasmonic Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes and Ti2C/Ag2S composites, the fabricated PEC biosensor exhibited satisfactory analysis capability for microRNA-155 detection. Moreover, the engineered plasmonic Au@Ag@CDs nanoenzymes act as multifunctional signal amplifiers to fabricate PEC biosensor and promote the development of plasmonic nanoenzymes.

Guihua Jiang: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Huimin Liu: Conceptualization, Validation. Jie Liu: Investigation. Li’e Liu: Methodology. Yuling Li: Validation, Investigation. Linsheng Xue: Formal analysis. Yongjun Wu: Conceptualization, Resources. Ruiying Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Guihua Jiang is currently a master candidate at College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, China. Her research interests focus on the photoelectrochemical detection of biomolecules.
Huimin Liu is currently a master candidate at College of Public Health, Zhengzhou University, China. His research interests focus on developing photoelectrochemical biosensors.
Jie Liu is currently a senior laboratory technician at College of Public Health, Zhengzhou University, China. Her research interests focus on developing photoelectrochemical probes.
Li’e Liu is currently a professor at College of Public Health, Zhengzhou University, China. Her research interests focus on developing fluorescent probes and food safety monitoring.
Yuling Li is currently a master candidate at College of Public Health, Zhengzhou University, China. Her research interests focus on developing the photoelectrochemical biosensing strategy.
Linsheng Xue is currently a master candidate at College of Public Health, Zhengzhou University, China. Her research interests focus on the preparation of functional nanomaterials.
Yongjun Wu is currently a professor at College of Public Health, Zhengzhou University, China. His research interests focus on screening and rapid detection of tumor biomarkers.

Ruiying Yang is currently an associate professor at College of Public Health, Zhengzhou University, China. Her research interests focus on the design and development of photoelectrochemical biosensor for detection and bioanalysis.
